risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Families were stratified by school district and randomly assigned to one of three conditions... Because of the small sample size, we used an urn randomization procedure to balance the groups on variables that might influence intervention outcomes..." Comment: This describes a systematic random allocation method (stratified random assignment with urn randomization), which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "Families were stratified by school district and randomly assigned to one of three conditions... Because of the small sample size, we used an urn randomization procedure to balance the groups on variables that might influence intervention outcomes" Comment: This describes random assignment and balancing, but it does not state any mechanism that concealed the upcoming allocation from the person enrolling families. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sealed opaque sequentially numbered envelopes, identical containers, or any other concealment method, so allocation concealment remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Families in the SFP and MSFP conditions were told that one condition included more activities to help parents cope with the stress of having an adolescent, but they did not know whether they were assigned to that condition or not." Comment: Participants were not fully blinded to the intervention content because the two active programs differed in visible session activities and the paper only indicates uncertainty about assignment to the added-stress-management condition, not successful blinding of participants or personnel. The facilitators also necessarily knew which program they delivered (standard SFP vs mindfulness-enhanced SFP with additional training), so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence parent-reported parenting and relationship outcomes, which are susceptible to performance and expectancy effects.

Summary: Outcomes were assessed by mothers and youths via mailed self-report surveys (“Youth and parent surveys were mailed separately”), and the study does not report any blinding of outcome assessors. Comment: Because the main outcomes were subjective questionnaire ratings of parenting and parent–youth relationship quality, the participants themselves were the assessors and their knowledge of allocation could influence responses; lack of blinding could therefore bias outcome measurement.

Quote: "Five of the 65 families in our sample did not complete assessments prior to the beginning of the intervention, and 24 families did not complete assessments after the end of the intervention." Comment: Although attrition is not trivial, the paper explicitly states that multiple imputation was used "to reduce any bias" from families who failed to complete pre- or post-intervention assessments and that analyses were based on all families in the sample. This is an appropriate and plausible missing-data approach for this pre/post RCT, so the incomplete outcome data are unlikely to have biased the results.
